history: The client has a history of substance abuse and has completed rehabilitation, indicating both vulnerability and resilience. He reports ongoing family difficulties, especially with his mother, characterized by rejection and emotional neglect. He has struggled with obesity since childhood and has experienced bullying related to his weight. These experiences likely contribute to feelings of shame, low self-worth, and difficulties with assertiveness and boundary-setting.
unlovable_belief: I am unlovable
I am bound to be abandoned
intermediate_belief: If I make a mistake or let people down, it means I am a failure and others will reject me. I must not show weakness or inadequacy, or people will see me as unworthy. If I don't meet expectations, I will confirm what others already think about me.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I keep failing or disappointing others, there is no hope for me to ever be accepted or valued.
coping_strategies: He tends to avoid situations that trigger anxiety or self-doubt, such as withdrawing or procrastinating after perceived failures. He may ruminate on past negative experiences and seek reassurance, but struggles to assert his needs due to fear of rejection. These strategies provide temporary relief but reinforce feelings of inadequacy and dependency.
situation: Missed an alarm and woke up late for work.
auto_thought: I can't even get up on time—I'm such a screw-up. Everyone at work will think I'm unreliable and useless.
behavior: He hesitates before getting out of bed, ruminates about what his coworkers will think, and considers calling in sick or making excuses. He may avoid checking his phone or contacting his workplace immediately.

They have become aware of similarities between their own approval-seeking behaviors and those of their father, who is described as persistently seeking approval and unconditional love from family members. unlovable_belief: I am unlovable
I am bound to be abandoned

intermediate_belief_depression: If I keep repeating these patterns and never get approval, I will always feel inadequate and disconnected.
coping_strategies: The patient seeks reassurance or approval from family members, sometimes modifying their actions to align with family expectations. They may also withdraw or avoid situations that could lead to further tension, attempting to manage anxiety but potentially reinforcing the cycle.

auto_thought: I'm just like my father—I can't stop needing their approval. If I don't get it, I'll never feel accepted.
behavior: I watch my father's interactions closely and compare them to my own. I try to act in ways that will please my family or avoid bringing up topics that might cause tension.

I am bound to be abandoned
intermediate_belief: If I don't work as hard or publish as much as my colleagues, then I am falling behind and will not succeed. I must always push myself to match or exceed others' achievements. My worth is measured by my productivity and recognition.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I can't keep up with my colleagues, then I will never be successful and there's no point in trying.
coping_strategies: The client tends to overwork, pushing themselves to work longer hours even when exhausted. They may procrastinate on tasks due to fear of not meeting high standards, and sometimes seek reassurance from peers or mentors like Kelly. These strategies temporarily reduce anxiety but reinforce the cycle of self-comparison and pressure.

auto_thought: I'm not doing enough compared to them. If I don't catch up, I'll fall behind and fail.

The return of physical symptoms coincided with starting a new relationship. These experiences have contributed to heightened vigilance about physical health and a tendency to ruminate on symptoms.
intermediate_belief: If I can't keep up with others because of my pain, they'll see me as weak or burdensome. If I try to act normal but my symptoms show, people will judge me or feel sorry for me. I must hide my struggles to be accepted.
intermediate_belief_depression: If my pain keeps coming back no matter what I do, there's no point in trying to enjoy things or connect with others.
coping_strategies: The client pushes themselves to attend social events despite pain, seeking normalcy and connection. They may avoid certain physical activities or withdraw from more demanding engagements to prevent symptom flare-ups. They sometimes seek reassurance from loved ones or medical professionals when symptoms worsen.
situation: Attending a Halloween party while in persistent pain.
auto_thought: Everyone will notice I'm struggling and think I'm weak. I can't keep up, and I'll ruin the fun for others.
behavior: Tries to hide signs of pain and act normal. Avoids more physically demanding activities at the party. Seeks reassurance from a close friend or partner about how they appear.

auto_thought: I don't know how to handle this. If I mess this up, it will prove I can't manage on my own.
